prompt: Share an untranslatable word or idiom from any language you know about — something that doesn't have a clean English equivalent — and explain what it captures.
response:
Japanese: 空気読めない (kuuki yomenai)  
Literal gloss: “can’t read the air”  

What it names  
The moment-by-moment social weather of a room—who is tense, who is waiting to speak, who is being politely ignored, what topic everyone silently agrees to skip.  
To “read the air” is to feel that invisible weather without being told.  
Someone who is kuuki yomenai (often abbreviated KY in text messages) is the person who cracks a loud joke while everyone else is mentally at a funeral, who keeps selling when the client already decided “no,” who asks the pregnant-looking woman when the baby is due—someone constitutionally blind to the unspoken.  

Why no English word fits  
English has “clueless,” “tactless,” “tone-deaf,” but each pinpoints a character flaw.  
Kuuki yomenai, by contrast, describes a temporary failure of perception, not a global verdict on the person; even otherwise sensitive people can “not read the air” in an unfamiliar culture or a sudden awkward silence.  
Conversely, the opposite praise 空気を読める (kuuki ga yomeru) is an everyday social superpower, the quiet engine that keeps Japanese classrooms, offices, and dinner tables running without explicit rules.  

So the loan-phrase “read the room” comes close, but it still feels like a skill you perform; kuuki yomenai is the sensation of crashing into a wall you didn’t realize was there.
